summary: DHH criticizes a rhetorical tactic where writers use the word 'really' as emphasis to make unsubstantiated claims sound authoritative. He highlights a PHP vs Python comparison where the author bolsters weak arguments by inserting 'really' as if it constitutes evidence. DHH finds this approach unconvincing and somewhat laughable, suggesting that genuine persuasion requires humor, wit, or actual substance rather than empty intensifiers.
key_points: Using 'really' as emphasis is a lazy substitute for actual evidence or argumentation
Unsubstantiated claims of bias require genuine wit or humor to be persuasive
The PHP vs Python debate example illustrates how weak arguments lean on rhetorical crutches
money_quotes: Trying to dress up unsubstantiated claims of bias as fact is hard enough as it is.
Slapping on the 'really' sticker has never cut it, though.
I mean, coooome on. I don't know whether to laugh or cry...
key_insight: Emphatic adverbs are not a substitute for evidence — if your argument needs the word 'really' to be convincing, it isn't.
